title: Berry Flux Equals Topological Charge
summary: For an isolated bad point, the integral of Berry curvature over a small surrounding sphere equals the integer topological charge of the node.
assumptions: The Berry connection is defined on the punctured sphere around the node.
regime: Abelian Berry geometry of an isolated node.
scope: Lecture One Berry-curvature restatement of node charge.

source_anchors.notes: The flux formula and dF=0 turn local geometry into a global sum rule.

representation: Surface-flux formula for node charge in terms of Berry curvature.
motivation: This note exists because it is the first explicit bridge from local Berry geometry to an integer topological invariant. It rewrites the Weyl-node charge as a curvature flux, so topology becomes something one can integrate instead of only define abstractly by degree.
prerequisite_story: Know the Berry-curvature two-form F on the punctured sphere around an isolated bad point and the fact that F over 2pi represents the first Chern class of the occupied-state line bundle. The sphere must not cross any other singularity.
local_context_story: In Witten Lecture One, this equation is the Berry-geometric restatement of the local Weyl-node charge. It is then combined with dF = 0 on the punctured Brillouin zone to recover the same zero-total-charge law as the Nielsen-Ninomiya theorem.
forward_links_story: This local flux formula feeds the punctured-zone Stokes argument for total charge cancellation and serves as the model for the later TKNN statement that a Chern number is a Berry-curvature integral over the whole Brillouin torus.
canonical_questions: Why is the flux integral over a small sphere integer-valued rather than continuously variable?
Why does this flux reproduce the same local charge as the degree-theory definition of a Weyl node?
How does dF = 0 turn the local flux formula into the global zero-sum law?
What assumptions fail if the sphere encloses more than one singular point or if the occupied subspace is not rank one?
scope_boundaries: This formula is written in the abelian rank-one setting around an isolated node. A multi-band occupied space requires the appropriate non-abelian generalization before taking gauge-invariant traces.
The enclosing sphere must lie in the regular region of the Brillouin zone and isolate the chosen singularity from all others.

math_expressions.notes: Berry-flux formula for the local node charge.
Berry curvature is closed away from the bad points.
Global charge-cancellation law obtained by summing the local fluxes on a compact punctured Brillouin zone.

symbols.meaning: small sphere surrounding the isolated node labeled by alpha
integer charge of the bad point

failure_modes: The simple abelian formula changes when multiple occupied bands require a non-abelian treatment.
retrieval_hints: Use when translating geometric Berry data into topological charge.
